revision.text:
[[ja:飯名神社]] {{Infobox religious building| | Name = '''Iina Jinja''' (飯名神社) | Image = [[File:Torii of Iina-jinja shrine (Tsukuba city,Ibaraki Prefecture).jpg|300px|Iina Jinja Torii (Tsukuba, Ibaraki Prefecture)]] | Location = {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|qid=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}} {{ill|Tsukuba|en|Tsukuba|qid=Q273863|lt=Tsukuba City|1=Tsukuba}} {{ill|Usui|Usui|Usui|2=ja|3=臼井 (つくば市)|lt=Usui|lt_ja=臼井|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usui|1=Usui, Tsukuba|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17194126}} 1-1 | Latitude = 36°12′18.7″ | Longitude = 140°5′33″ | Deity = {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|qid=Q2280323|lt=Ukimomochi|1=Ukemochi}}
{{ill|Ichikishima-hime|Ichikishima-hime|Ichikishima-hime|2=ja|3=市杵嶋姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}} | Sacred Obj = Female Stone (Iina Benten) | Status = {{ill|Shrine Outside the Ranked System|Schrein außerhalb des Rangsystems|Shrine Outside the Ranked System|2=ja|3=式外社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Outside the Ranked System|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳|comment=changed title based on wikidata|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qid=Q11064932}}
Formerly {{ill|Non-Ranked Shrine|Nicht klassifiziert|Non-Ranked Shrine|2=ja|3=無格社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Non-Ranked Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}} | Foundation = Unknown | Main Hall = One-room shrine, built in {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|qid=Q210684|lt=Ryūzō style|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | Alt. Names = Iina’s Benten
Usui’s Benten | Marker = | Annual Fest= Old New Year Hatsu-mi | Rituals = | Map = Japan Ibaraki |image = File:Torii of Iina-jinja shrine (Tsukuba city,Ibaraki Prefecture).jpg | prefecture= {{ill|Tsukuba|qid=Q273863|1=Tsukuba}} | coordinates= {{Coord|36.205194|N|140.0925|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity= {{ill|Ichikishimahime|qid=Q11059454|1=Ichikishima-hime}}; {{ill|Ukemochi|qid=Q2280323|1=Ukemochi}} }} '''Iina Jinja''' (飯名神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|qid=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}} {{ill|Tsukuba|en|Tsukuba|qid=Q273863|lt=Tsukuba City|1=Tsukuba}} {{ill|Usui|Usui|Usui|2=ja|3=臼井 (つくば市)|lt=Usui|lt_ja=臼井|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usui|1=Usui, Tsukuba|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17194126}}. It is an ancient shrine ({{ill|Shrine Outside the Ranked System|Schrein außerhalb des Rangsystems|Shrine Outside the Ranked System|2=ja|3=式外社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Outside the Ranked System|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳|comment=changed title based on wikidata|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qid=Q11064932}}) mentioned as “Iina Kami” in ''{{ill|Hitachi Fudoki|Hitachi Fudoki|Hitachi Fudoki|2=ja|3=常陸国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Fudoki|1=Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11481687}}''. In the Middle Ages it was called “Iinano Jinja” or “Inano Miya”, and today it is affectionately known as “Benten of Inano (Iina, Usui)”. Under the former Modern Shrine Rank System ({{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qid=Q712534|lt=Rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}), its Rank was that of a non-Ranked shrine. == Overview == It is a shrine devoted to megalithic worship located on the southern slopes of {{ill|Mount Tsukuba|en|Mount Tsukuba|qid=Q1754619|lt=Mount Tsukuba|1=Mount Tsukuba}}. The Minano River—which originates from the Gokōgahara situated between the twin peaks of Mount Tsukuba—flows to the west of the shrine grounds. A clear stream nearby is also used as a site for money washing. The shrine building is oriented with its back toward the north-northeast, where the twin peaks of Mount Tsukuba stand. The shrine grounds lie in the Usui district at an elevation of about 80 meters to the northwest. From the “Iina Jinja Entrance” bus stop at {{ill|Tsukutaku|Tsukutaku|Tsukutaku|2=ja|3=つくタク|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukutaku|1=Tsukutaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17215342}}, a paved approach with signage leads to the shrine, although it is a steep and difficult residential road. This road continues alongside the shrine building; taking the Y-shaped fork to the east leads in front of the first torii of {{ill|Tsukubasan Shrine|en|Tsukubasan Shrine|qid=Q11602876|lt=Tsukuba Shrine|1=Tsukubasan Shrine}} (the large torii of the sixth block), and going straight passes by Tsukisuiishi Shrine before merging onto {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture Route 139|Ibaraki Prefecture Route 139|Ibaraki Prefecture Route 139|2=ja|3=茨城県道139号筑波山公園線|lt=Ibaraki Route 139|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ibaraki Prefecture Route 139|1=Ibaraki Prefectural Road Route 139|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11617553}}. There is no parking lot on the premises, though a turnaround is possible during off-peak seasons. Originally a shrine of the Ukamochi deity group, during the Edo period a Benzaiten ({{ill|Benzaiten|en|Benzaiten|qid=Q818468|qid=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten|1=Benzaiten}}) shrine ({{ill|Ichikishima-hime|Ichikishima-hime|Ichikishima-hime|2=ja|3=市杵嶋姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}) was constructed, after which the worship of Benzaiten grew stronger; even today it is fondly called “Benten of Inano”, “Benten of Iina”, and “Benten of Usui”. == Deities == [[File:The Haiden of Iina-jinja shrine (Tsukuba city,Ibaraki prefecture).jpg|thumb|Haiden of Iina Jinja (August 2018)]] ; Main Deities : {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|qid=Q2280323|lt=Ukimomochi|1=Ukemochi}} : {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|Ichikishima-hime|Ichikishima-hime|2=ja|3=イチキシマヒメ|lt=Ichikishima-hime|lt_ja=市杵嶋姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}} ; Auxiliary Deities : {{ill|Izanagi|en|Izanagi|qid=Q813858|lt=Izanagi|1=Izanagi}}, {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qid=Q272993|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|qid=Q276944|qid=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}}, {{ill|Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayamahiko|Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayamahiko|Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayamahiko|2=ja|3=金山彦神|lt=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime|lt=Kanayamahiko|lt_ja=金山比古命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanayamahiko|qq=}} * The {{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|qid=Q327532|lt=Sacred Object|1=Shintai}} is a megalith ({{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|qid=Q11587880|lt=Iwa-za|1=Iwakura rock}}) with the shrine building and the {{ill|Shrine Office|Shrine Office|Shrine Office|2=ja|3=社務所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Office|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社|1=Shinto shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q845945}} located on a flat area on its southern side. Separate from this sacred object, a one‐room main hall built in the Ryūzō style and an auxiliary building have been constructed. * The megalith is called the “female stone” due to its large fissure. On top of the female stone is a small subsidiary shrine and a standing stone enshrining the Inari deity, which is referred to as the “male stone”. By the contrast of yin and yang, the female stone is identified with Benzaiten, and it is also known by the epithet '''Iina Benten'''. There are many other megaliths on the shrine grounds adorned with {{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|qid=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}}. * It is venerated as a deity of abundant harvest and prosperous business. === Auxiliary Enshrined Deities === The record of the enshrinement of the auxiliary deities is not mentioned in the “Iina Jinja Origins” (see below). {{ill|Izanagi|en|Izanagi|qid=Q813858|lt=Izanagi|1=Izanagi}} and {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} belong to the Tsukuba Shrine Second Group. Moreover, among the independent shrines ({{ill|Independent Shrine|Independent Shrine|Independent Shrine|2=ja|3=単立|redirects to 単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Independent Shrine|qq=}}) in the area of the former Usui Village, several shrines have no record of a merger with {{ill|Susomiya Shrine|Susomiya Shrine|Susomiya Shrine|2=ja|3=蚕影神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susomiya Shrine|1=Kokage Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17228464}}, which served as the village shrine for the former Tai Village and as a repository for the consolidation of many shrines ({{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|en|Shrine Consolidation Policy|qid=Q11590700|lt=Shrine Arrangement|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}}). * '''Usui Yatsuka Shrine'''. Located on the northeastern mountainside of the Usui district. In Usui, the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|en|Gion Matsuri|qid=Q979873|lt=Gion Festival|1=Gion Matsuri}} is held as a summer festival, and on the day before the festival, a sacred fire is kindled at Yatsuka Shrine. * '''Tate no Zao Shrine'''. The sacred object is a statue of {{ill|Zaō Gongen|lt=Zaō Gongen|lt=Zao Gongen|Zaō Gongen|lt=Zaō Gongen|lt=Zao Gongen|Zaō Gongen|lt=Zaō Gongen|lt=Zao Gongen|2=ja|3=蔵王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zao Gongen|qq=}} designated as a tangible cultural property (sculpture) of Tsukuba City. Originally enshrined at the summit of Mount Zao in Kodaka, {{ill|Tsuchiura|en|Tsuchiura|qid=Q653446|lt=Tsuchiura City|1=Tsuchiura}}, it was relocated in Tenshō 3 (1575) following the fall of {{ill|Oda Castle|en|Oda Castle|qid=Q11463019|lt=Odajō|1=Oda Castle}} by Kodaka Chikō to Tate no. The original Zao Gongen has, despite the influence of quarrying on Mount Zao, continued to exist as “Kanayama Shrine” under the Shrine Agency in Tsuchiura City (Kodaka, Ishizaki). The enshrined deity is unknown. * '''Usui’s Oyamao-sama'''. There once existed a shrine called Oyamao-sama that enshrined {{ill|Sannō Gongen|Sannō Gongen|Sannō Gongen|2=ja|3=山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. === Shrines Within the Grounds === There are four subsidiary shrines: Inari Shrine, Atago Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, and Yamanokami Shrine. === Hatsu-mi Festival === The annual festival is the Hatsu-mi Festival held on the first day of the New Year (around February). In addition to typical food stalls, traditional peddlers ({{ill|Peddler|en|Peddler|qid=Q638172|lt=Peddling|1=Peddler}}) selling miscellaneous goods such as Tsukuba mandarins, seafood, bamboo crafts, and agricultural supplies gather. Moreover, the {{ill|Daruma Market|Daruma Market|Daruma Market|2=ja|3=だるま市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daruma Market|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to だるま|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to だるま|1=Daruma doll|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q276107}}—a talisman market linked to Benzaiten worship—is a local specialty. == Origins == An inscription titled “Iina Jinja Origins” is displayed on the side of the worship hall under the name of the Iina Jinja Office, detailing the deities, history, and annual festival. Unless otherwise noted, the account in “Iina Jinja Origins” is followed. === Foundation === The founding is unknown, and no record appears in the documents of the former Usui Village. === 'Hitachi Fudoki' and 'Man'yōshū' === In the clause for Shinta District in ''{{ill|Hitachi Fudoki|Hitachi Fudoki|Hitachi Fudoki|2=ja|3=常陸国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Fudoki|1=Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11481687}}'', there is an interpretation that the “Iina Kami” mentioned— “There is a shrine of Iina to the west of that village; this is a branch of the Iina Kami enshrined on Mount Tsukuba”—refers to this shrine. According to this view, the founding dates back to before the 8th century, making it one of the oldest shrines on Mount Tsukuba. In ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|qid=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū|1=Man'yōshū}}'', in the eastern poem of volume 14— “Snow may fall upon the Tsukuba peaks; perhaps the beloved child’s cloth dries”—the term “inawokamo (伊奈乎可母)” is interpreted as a pun on “inaokamo (飯名岡, 飯奈岡, 稲岡)” via {{ill|Kakekotoba|en|Kakekotoba|qid=Q939895|lt=Kakekotoba|1=Kakekotoba}}. In both ''Ibaraki Daikan'' and ''Tsukuba-gun Annai-ki'', it is stated that “in Usui it is Iinaoka,” and the registered address in the Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Agency’s register is “Usui Inaka 1-ban”, suggesting that “inawokamo” survived as a subdivision name. Miyamoto Shin'ichi, in ''Tsukuba Rekishi Sanpo'' (published in Shōwa 43), theorized that the shrine was founded as the village shrine of Tsukuba’s deities—revered as the sacred mount ({{ill|Sacred mountains|en|Sacred mountains|qid=Q1595289|lt=Sacred Mount|1=Sacred mountains}})—and is of the same class as the Rokusho Shrines. (“Rokusho Shrine” is the honorary name for the shrine formerly known as {{ill|Rokusho Shrine|Rokusho Shrine|Rokusho Shrine|2=ja|3=六所神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokusho Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579194|1=Rokusho Jingu}} in the Rokusho district until the end of the Meiji period.) === Iinano Jinja Chronicle and Inano Miya Wani-kuchi === Historical documents include the “Iinano Jinja Chronicle” from the Rokusho Shrine chief priest Nagato-ke records and an inscription of {{ill|Waniguchi|en|Waniguchi|qid=Q11674315|lt=Wani-kuchi|1=Waniguchi}} (Inano Miya Wani-kuchi) unearthed near Benkei Nanamodori on the mid-slope of Mount Tsukuba. The “Iina Jinja Origins” includes the following entries: * “Our shrine’s enshrinement: In Kōsei 2 (1456), on November 16, a shrine was erected at Iinano and Hojokami was enshrined.” ({{ill|Kōshō|en|Kōshō|qid=Q1143364|lt=Kōsei|1=Kōshō}}) * “Hitachi Province, Hokujō District, Usui Village, Inano Miya Wani-kuchi, Danna Group, {{ill|Bunmei|en|Bunmei|qid=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei|1=Bunmei}} 11 (1479), May 25” (inscription of Inano Miya Wani-kuchi). * “Tenshō 19 (1591), on the first day of the New Year (Hatsu-mi), the shrine was relocated” ({{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|qid=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}; {{ill|Sengū|en|Sengū|qid=Q2271023|lt=Shrine Relocation|1=Sengū}}). * “Manji 3 (1660), on March 14, Benzaiten was constructed and the enshrined deity was Ichikishima-hime” ({{ill|Manji (era)|en|Manji (era)|qid=Q1145295|lt=Manji|1=Manji (era)}}). It is evident that in Kōsei 2 (1456) {{ill|Ukamochi|Ukamochi|Ukamochi|2=ja|3=保食神 (宇気母知神)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukamochi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was enshrined at Iinano through an invitation ({{ill|Invitation for Enshrinement|Invitation for Enshrinement|Invitation for Enshrinement|2=ja|3=勧請|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Invitation for Enshrinement|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 分霊|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 分霊|1=Bunrei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q195793}}), and that a shrine relocation ({{ill|Sengū|en|Sengū|qid=Q2271023|lt=Shrine Relocation|1=Sengū}}) took place in Tenshō 19 (1591). The phrase “our shrine’s enshrinement” signifies the establishment of Iinano Jinja with Hojokami as its principal deity. If this is interpreted as the foundation, then continuity with the “Iina Jinja (Iinano Jinja)” mentioned as “Iina Kami” in ''Hitachi Fudoki'' is lacking. However, these records also attest to the existence of a place called “Iinano” in Usui Village of Hokujō District, which became the shrine’s name. Thus, one may regard it either as a trace of faith connected to Iina Kami or as a transformation from sacred mountain worship. The term “shrine relocation” may refer not only to the movement from a temporary hall used during shrine repairs but also to the transfer of the enshrinement (relocation of the seat). Consequently, “Iinano” and “inawoka (稲岡)” might have been neighboring areas distinguished merely by the characters “野” (field) and “岡” (hill). In a diagram from “{{ill|Bunka|en|Bunka|qid=Q1373146|lt=Bunka|1=Bunka}}” 5 (1808) in ''Hitachi Tsukubayama Engi'', between the inscriptions “'''Benten'''” and “'''Usui Village'''” (indicating Iina Jinja) the characters “'''Iko no'''” are written. The description “Benzaiten construction” suggests that initially, {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|Ichikishima-hime|Ichikishima-hime|2=ja|3=市杵嶋姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}} was not enshrined in the main hall but was instead enshrined in a separate subsidiary shrine. === Benzaiten Worship === Although Iina Jinja enshrines Ukamochi and Ichikishima-hime as its principal deities, the worship of Benzaiten—established in Manji 3 (1660)—became dominant, and even today the shrine is affectionately known as “Benten of Inano”, “Benten of Iina”, and “Benten of Usui”. It also features unique customs such as money washing, the bestowal of lucky seed money, and the Hatsu-mi Festival. It is primarily a shrine of Benzaiten worship rather than of Inari. In the Benzaiten of the Uga deity lineage ({{ill|Ugajin|en|Ugajin|qid=Q795080|lt=Uga Deity|1=Ugajin}}), owing to its snake-deity worship, the Hatsu-mi day is given special prominence and ceremonies are held. Moreover, the divine messenger ({{ill|Shinshi|en|Shinshi|qid=Q10444973|lt=Divine Messenger|1=Shinshi}}) of Uga Benzaiten is regarded as the white snake ({{ill|White Snake|White Snake|White Snake|2=ja|3=白蛇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/White Snake|1=white snake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q5367369}}). The “Iina Jinja Origins” links the annual Hatsu-mi Festival to the shrine relocation (New Year’s Hatsu-mi Day), suggesting that Hatsu-mi was revered even before Ichikishima-hime’s service. === Since the Meiji Period === The time when the shrine was renamed from “Iinano Jinja (Inano Miya)” to “Iina Jinja” is unknown. Under the Modern Shrine Rank System, it did not possess an official Rank. On January 23, Shōwa 27 (1952), a {{ill|Religious Corporation|Religious Corporation|Religious Corporation|2=ja|3=宗教法人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Religious Corporation|1=religious corporation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q7311343}} was established. In Heisei 24 (2012), reconstruction work following the damage from the {{ill|Aftermath of the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|en|Aftermath of the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|qid=Q1136168|lt=Great East Japan Earthquake|1=Aftermath of the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}} was completed, which included the restoration of the {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|qid=Q234731|lt=Torii|1=Torii}}, stone steps, and stone walls. A commemorative stone monument was erected in honor of the reconstruction. == Description in 'Hitachi Fudoki' == “Iina Kami” is a divine name found only in the clause for {{ill|Shinta District|Shinta District|Shinta District|2=ja|3=信太郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinta District|1=Shida district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11386034}} in ''{{ill|Hitachi Fudoki|Hitachi Fudoki|Hitachi Fudoki|2=ja|3=常陸国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Fudoki|1=Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11481687}}''. The clause for {{ill|Tsukuba District, Ibaraki|en|Tsukuba District, Ibaraki|qid=Q7850092|lt=Tsukuba District|1=Tsukuba District, Ibaraki}} in ''Hitachi Fudoki'' does not mention the main shrine, and subsequent documents such as {{ill|Rikkokushi|en|Rikkokushi|qid=Q1151168|lt=Rokukokushi|1=Rikkokushi}} (including ''{{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|qid=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}'') do not reference it. Therefore, “Iina Kami” can be considered a unique {{ill|Shrine Outside the Ranked System|Shrine Outside the Ranked System|Shrine Outside the Ranked System|2=ja|3=式外社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Outside the Ranked System|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳|comment=changed title based on wikidata|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qid=Q11064932}} whose existence is indirectly attested solely by the description of a branch shrine in Shinta District. === Iina Shrine of Shinta District === The Iina Shrine of Shinta District is, like “Iina Kami”, a shrine outside the Ranked system. Regarding its equivalent shrine, ''Shinpen Hitachi Fudoki'' states in its text, “It is said to be the current Inari of Onagahara” and “Iina and Inari are colloquially similar, so later generations strongly regarded them as Inari,” citing {{ill|Ryūgasaki, Ibaraki|en|Ryūgasaki, Ibaraki|qid=Q844138|lt=Ryūgasaki City|1=Ryūgasaki, Ibaraki}} and {{ill|Namazai Village|Namazai Village|Namazai Village|2=ja|3=馴柴村|lt=Namauta Town|lt_ja=馴馬町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namazai Village|1=Nareshiba|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11668151}}’s {{ill|Onagahime Shrine|Onagahime Shrine|Onagahime Shrine|2=ja|3=女化神社|lt=Onagainari Shrine|lt_ja=女化稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onagahime Shrine|1=Onabake Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11446317}}. It is later amended with statements such as “Upon further review, the ruins of Iina Kami are to be identified as Inazuka” and “In the Fudoki, the branch of Tsukuba’s Iina Kami is unmistakable.” The ''Dai Nihon Chimei Jisho'' concurs with the Inazuka theory, stating “Iina Shrine is, in fact, Inazuka of {{ill|Yatsushiro Village|Yatsushiro Village|Yatsushiro Village|2=ja|3=八代村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsushiro Village|1=yatsushiromura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11390546}}, and the name Inashiki is synonymous with Inayatsushiro,” as argued in works like Kongokō and Shinkokushi. An annotated edition also notes the similarity of place names with {{ill|Kashima District, Ibaraki|en|Kashima District, Ibaraki|qid=Q6374307|lt=Kashima District|1=Kashima District, Ibaraki}} and {{ill|Tokushuku Village|Tokushuku Village|Tokushuku Village|2=ja|3=徳宿村|lt=Iina Village|lt_ja=飯名村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushuku Village|1=Tokushuku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11489595}}. Inazuka (Iko-tsuka) is the “Inazuka Kofun” located in Yatsushiro-cho, {{ill|Ryūgasaki, Ibaraki|en|Ryūgasaki, Ibaraki|qid=Q844138|lt=Ryūgasaki City|1=Ryūgasaki, Ibaraki}}. It is an earthen mound ({{ill|Enpun|Enpun|Enpun|2=ja|3=円墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enpun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Round Kofun|qid=Q11394747}}) situated in an alluvial plain ({{ill|Alluvial plain|en|Alluvial plain|qid=Q656586|lt=Alluvial Land|1=Alluvial plain}}), and a public road ({{ill|Public Road|Public Road|Public Road|2=ja|3=官道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Public Road|1=kandō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11452141}}) is said to have passed nearby. Local histories, such as those found in ''{{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|en|Wamyō Ruijushō|qid=Q2702542|lt=Wamyō Ruijushō|1=Wamyō Ruijushō}}'', attribute the origin of the name “Inashiki” (or “Inashikori”) to Iina Kami. * ''Shinpen Hitachi Fudoki'' states: “Even if Inashiki is used as a place name, it originates from Iina Kami; the term ‘shiki’ (敷) is akin to words for mansion or warehouse, deriving from the grounds of Iina Shrine. In general, ‘shikichi’ means a plain.” Thus, “Inashiki” is interpreted as meaning “the grounds of Iina Shrine.” * ''Dai Nihon Chimei Jisho'', citing the {{ill|Tonegawa Zushi|Tonegawa Zushi|Tonegawa Zushi|2=ja|3=利根川図志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tonegawa Zushi|qq=}}, states: “Iina means that which later came to be identified with rice, and Inashiki denotes the land on which the deity of Iina resided.” The earlier notion that “the name Inashiki is synonymous with Inayatsushiro” implies that the shrine’s grounds became known as “Inashiki” while its shrine (yashiro) took the old village name “Yatsushiro.” * ''Hyōchū Kokufudoki'' records, “According to Miyamoto Motokyu (from Kongokō), there is an Inazuka in the village, which the locals also call Tsukubayama. Inazuka is approximately the same as Iina-zuka.” These documents also indicate that the name “Iina” has invariably transformed into “ine” in all instances, including in “inawoka” and “Inanano.” The place name of Inashiki in Shinta District, originating from Tsukuba Mountain’s “Iina Kami”, became the basis for names such as {{ill|Inashiki District, Ibaraki|en|Inashiki District, Ibaraki|qid=Q1154673|lt=Inashiki District|1=Inashiki District, Ibaraki}} and the Inashiki Plateau—established in 1896 ({{ill|1896|en|1896|qid=Q7846|lt=1896|1=1896}}; {{ill|1896|en|1896|qid=Q7846|lt=Meiji 29|1=1896}}) through the merger of Shinta District and {{ill|Kawachi District|Kawachi District|Kawachi District|2=ja|3=河内郡 (茨城県)|lt=Kawachi District|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi District|1=Kōchi district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11553459}}, and this naming was carried over in the formation of {{ill|Inashiki|en|Inashiki|qid=Q838675|lt=Inashiki City|1=Inashiki}} during the Great Heisei Merger ({{ill|Great Heisei Merger|Great Heisei Merger|Great Heisei Merger|2=ja|3=平成の大合併|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Heisei Merger|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to Japanese cities and townsの廃置分合|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to Japanese cities and townsの廃置分合|1=Municipal mergers and dissolutions in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2411657}}). === The Deity of Iina Shrine === Atop the Inazuka Kofun stands a small shrine known as Inashiki Jinja. Note that the Inazuka Kofun is on private land, and the Inashiki Jinja atop it is also a private, non-public shrine; consequently, visits by outsiders (including local survey committee members) are not accepted. Although Inashiki Jinja is a private shrine on private land, the pre-war ''{{ill|Shinja Meisaichō|Shinja Meisaichō|Shinja Meisaichō|2=ja|3=神社明細帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinja Meisaichō|1=jinjameisaichou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11590704}}'' lists it as “Inazuka, Yatsushiro Village, Inashiki District” and as a non-Ranked shrine. Official documents regarding temples and shrines in Ryūgasaki City note “founding and enshrined deity unknown” and state that it “collapsed in Taishō 10 (1921) and was later rebuilt”. According to ''Shinpen Hitachi Fudoki'', Inazuka has two shrines—the “Tsukuba Gongen” shrine and the “Ichiyo Inari” shrine: * “The common people of Inazuka refer to this mound as Tsukubayama; above it stands a small shrine for '''Tsukuba Gongen'''. About sixty years ago, the mound was burned; although there were large trees, they withered and were cut down by the owner, who resided near the mound. The small stone shrine atop the mound was subsequently built by the owner after the fire, and it came to be regarded as a divine relic of Tsukuba.” * “A short distance behind the mound stands an Ichiyo Pine—a great tree with two enclosures; its leaves are all of one kind, and beneath it there is a small shrine known as '''Ichiyo Inari'''.” Memories concerning Iina Shrine are reflected in the traditions of “Tsukuba’s Divine Relics”. In Taishō 5 (1916), the ''Inashiki District Gazetteer'' records that a small shrine called either Inashiki Jinja or Tsukuba Jinja once stood in the middle of a rice field in the eastern part of Yatsushiro Village, but it burned down around 1840. However, the account in ''Shinpen Hitachi Fudoki''—based on the tradition of “Tsukuba’s Divine Relics”—suggests that a stone shrine for Tsukuba Gongen was erected after the fire, so it is unclear whether a small shrine existed before that. The current state is unknown, although an article in “Kōhō Inashiki” features a photograph (of indeterminate date) of a small shrine (a shrine building rather than a stone shrine) from the Ryūgasaki City Museum of History and Folklore. As for the Ichiyo Inari beneath the Ichiyo Pine at Inazuka, no origins or details are recorded, and its current condition is also unknown. If Inazuka is regarded as “Tsukubayama”, then the Ichiyo Inari can be seen as analogous to a “Iina Shrine” at the foothills. There are many uncertainties regarding the current two shrines at Inazuka, and it is considered difficult to ascertain the ancient “Iina Kami”. == Surrounding Shrines == '''Gassui Ishi Shrine''' (がっすいせきじんじゃ) : Located in Tsukuba, {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|qid=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}} – Tsukuba (formerly Tsukuba Village, Tsukuba District) : Enshrined Deity – {{ill|Iwanaga-hime|en|Iwanaga-hime|qid=Q8191705|lt=Iwanagahime|1=Iwanaga-hime}} : Annual Festival – 25th day of the lunar New Year : Independent Shrine – {{ill|Independent Shrine|Independent Shrine|Independent Shrine|2=ja|3=単立|redirects to 単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|lt=Independent Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Independent Shrine|qq=}} * It enshrines a megalith—according to legend, once a month red water flows from a central hole—as its sacred object. Although it is an independent shrine, it is open to public worship and is widely venerated for its reputed power to bestow children and to cure women’s ailments. * According to the “Gassui Ishi Shrine Origins” displayed on the side of the shrine building’s covering, this locale is the sacred site of the passing of Iwanagahime. Iwanagahime was the fourth child of the twin deities of Tsukubayama (the two deities known as Nakkaku Nison) and, unable to climb due to illness, passed away here. Later, by command of the twin deities, she became the guardian of the common people. (This genealogy, which designates the twin deities as the parent deities of Iwanagahime, is unique and differs from that found in the {{ill|Kojiki|Kojiki|Kojiki|2=ja|3=記紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|comment=enwiki is a redirect|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}.) * The stream flowing through the shrine grounds is the “men and women river”, originating near the Gokōgahara between the twin peaks of Mount Tsukuba. Downstream, there is Iina Jinja, which shares characteristics with the men and women river, the worship of the megalith ({{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|qid=Q11587880|lt=Iwa-za|1=Iwakura rock}}), the worship of the yin stone, and the goddess worship. * At Susomiya Shrine in Jingun-yama, across the Sakakogawa (also known as Sakeka River), the younger sister deity, {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|Konohanasakuya-hime|Konohanasakuya-hime|2=ja|3=コノハナサクヤヒメ|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|lt_ja=木花咲耶姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}}, is enshrined. * In Heisei 21 (2009), the shrine building was renovated. == Footnotes == [http://www.joyoliving.co.jp/index.html Jōyō Living] : [http://www.joyoliving.co.jp/special/historical/kennan/kennan00106.htm Tsukuba City: The Yin-Yang Stone of Iina Jinja]. Accessed November 1, 2013. The designation of the deities of Tsukuba Shrine Second Group as the two deities (Nakkaku Nison) was made during the Meiji period. Tsukuba Foothills Green Tourism Promotion Council, “[http://www.tsukuba-gt.sakura.ne.jp/archives/category/susomiroku/ Tsukuba Foothills Regional Information Paper ‘Susomiroku’]” Vol. 32, Spring/Summer 2015. April 1, 2015. Accessed April 12, 2016. According to a stone monument titled “Kanayama Shrine History” inscribed in the name of the Kodaka family of Kanayama Shrine on its grounds (dated July, Heisei 10 [1998]). The founding is recorded as Tenshō 2 (1574), when Kodaka Chikō enshrined a deity statue from Zao Gongen of Mount Kanamine and installed it at the summit of Mount Zao in Ishizaki. After relocating the sacred object, an ancient tree venerated as Kanayama Shrine was maintained, but with recent shrine restoration works a new stone statue was carved. Ministry of the Environment, Yusui Conservation Portal, [https://www.env.go.jp/water/yusui/result/sub4-2/PRE8-4-2.html Representative Springs of Ibaraki Prefecture]. The overview of “Oyamao’s Well” in Usui, Tsukuba City, notes that a shrine called Oyamao-sama once existed near the well. Accessed April 12, 2016. It was abolished at the end of the Meiji period and merged into Susomiya Shrine in Tsukuba City’s Jingun district. The seven enshrined deities remain venerated at Susomiya Shrine. Uga Deity is {{Iwanaga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|飯名神社|version=103510105|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:飯名神社]] {{wikidata link|Q17192747}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tsukuba]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tsukuba]] [[Category:Shikigesha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
